attributes.stem:
A 31-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a two-month history of progressive shortness of breath on exertion, palpitations and fatigue. He has a medical history of asthma. His medications include salbutamol inhaler as needed. His father died suddenly at the age of 38 and his paternal grandmother had a heart condition related to a murmur.

On examination, he appears mildly distressed. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Cardiac examination reveals a systolic ejection murmur that decreases in intensity when squatting. A third heart sound and a prominent jugular venous 'a wave' are noted. His electrocardiogram shows prominent Q waves in leads II, III, aVF, V5 and V6. Chest X-ray demonstrates an enlarged cardiac silhouette. Echocardiography confirms asymmetrical septal hypertrophy with left ventricular outflow tract gradient of 35 mmHg at rest. A diagnosis of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is made.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is an autosomal dominant genetic disease characterised by thickening of the left ventricular wall. This patient presents with classic symptoms of exertional dyspnoea, palpitations, and fatigue, along with physical examination findings consistent with HCM including a systolic ejection murmur that decreases with squatting. The echocardiogram confirms the diagnosis with asymmetrical septal hypertrophy and left ventricular outflow tract obstruction.

The most important aspect of HCM management is assessing the risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD). Major risk factors include:

  • family history of SCD in a first-degree relative under 50 years old

  • severe left ventricular hypertrophy (>30 mm)

  • recent unexplained syncope

  • left ventricular apical aneurysm

  • left ventricular systolic dysfunction

This patient has a significant family history of his father dying suddenly at age 38, which places him at high risk for SCD. Therefore, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator is the most appropriate initial management to prevent SCD.

Ramipril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor that is contraindicated in HCM, particularly in patients with outflow tract obstruction. Vasodilators such as ACE inhibitors reduce afterload, which can worsen the outflow tract gradient and exacerbate symptoms. This would be an inappropriate and potentially harmful choice for this patient.

Aspirin has no role in the primary management of HCM. It would only be indicated if there were comorbid conditions requiring antiplatelet therapy, such as coronary artery disease or atrial fibrillation with low stroke risk. There is no evidence in this case to suggest these conditions are present.

Furosemide is a loop diuretic that would not be appropriate as initial management for HCM. Diuretics can potentially worsen symptoms by reducing preload, which can exacerbate outflow tract obstruction. They may be used cautiously in patients with HCM who have concomitant heart failure with fluid overload, but this is not the case for this patient.

Referral for cardiac transplantation is an extreme intervention reserved for end-stage HCM with refractory heart failure symptoms despite maximal medical therapy and other interventions. This patient has newly diagnosed HCM without evidence of end-stage disease, making transplantation inappropriate as initial management.

In addition to the appropriate intervention, patients with HCM should receive counselling regarding physical activity limitations. While complete restriction from competitive sports was previously recommended, current guidelines allow for moderate-intensity exercise on an individual basis, avoiding high-intensity activities that could trigger arrhythmias.
learningPoints: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) patients with a family history of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in a first-degree relative under 50 years should receive an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator for primary prevention.
Vasodilators such as angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are contraindicated in HCM as they reduce afterload and may worsen left ventricular outflow tract obstruction.
references: Ellims AH. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in the adolescent. Aust Fam Physician. 2017;46(8):553-557. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28787553/

Ommen SR, Mital S, Burke MA, Day SM, Deswal A, Elliott P, et al. 2020 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Patients With Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines. Circulation. 2020 Dec 22;142(25):e558-e631. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33215931/

Link MS. Are Beta-Blockers Effective for Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy? NEJM Journal Watch Cardiology. 2022. Available at: https://www.jwatch.org/na54411/2022/01/03/are-beta-blockers-effective-hypertrophic-cardiomyopathy

Robinson K, Frenneaux MP, Stockins B, Karatasakis G, Poloniecki JD, McKenna WJ. Atrial fibrillation in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: a longitudinal study. J Am Coll Cardiol. 1990 May;15(6):1279-85. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2329232/

Elliott PM, Anastasakis A, Borger MA, Borggrefe M, Cecchi F, Charron P, et al. 2014 ESC Guidelines on diagnosis and management of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: the Task Force for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy of the European Society of Cardiology (ESC). European Heart Journal. 2014;35(39):2733-79. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25173338/
